summary: Most OAuth discovery/JWKS scope is implemented and the provided deterministic checks passed, but the implementation advertises an MCP OAuth endpoint at /mcp/oauth in protected-resource metadata and the /config connection example while no app/mcp/oauth route exists. That means the metadata/guide are not fully aligned with actual runtime routes, and the @oauth-discovery test only asserts the advertised string instead of proving the route exists.
satisfied_exit_criteria: Discovery route handlers exist for /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource, /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server, /.well-known/openid-configuration, and /oauth/jwks.
Metadata is internally consistent for issuer, authorization endpoint, token endpoint, JWKS URI, grants, response types, auth method, and scope format.
JWKS exposes a public RS256 key without private key fields and the E2E test verifies an issued client_credentials token signature with it.
Connection guide includes the metadata and JWKS endpoints.
Provided deterministic summary reports npm run test:unit, npm run test:e2e -- --grep @oauth-discovery, and npm run verify all passed.
missing_requirements: Protected-resource metadata and the /config MCP OAuth connection example advertise /mcp/oauth, but the repository currently has app/mcp, app/mcp/none, and app/mcp/basic only. This violates alignment with actual runtime routes and makes the guide example partially inaccurate.
The @oauth-discovery E2E test does not catch the missing advertised MCP OAuth route; it only checks that /mcp/oauth appears in metadata/config text.
human_review_triggers: Required checks do not prove the claimed behavior.
Implementation changes contradict the product spec or security/reliability docs.
